speaker: interviewer
text: [? --'95 ?] We're doing an interview with survivor, Dr. Esther Fox. And the interviewer's name is [? Marion ?] [? Rabner. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were in Rouen. And you went to Rouen because your brother, Isak was there, or he traveled with you to Rouen?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, he remained to study his-- his subject. I did it myself, with a-- a colleague. We went-- yeah. I don't remember how we got together. She went to Rouen too. And over there we moved into a-- also a hotel, where already recommended us that they are already Polish student, and they're from-- were even from Russia, somewhere. So we moved in. And we started and this for a whole year.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you stayed one year?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Isak stayed longer?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, he didn't come.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, he didn't come.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was in Nancy. He didn't move at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, he was in Nancy.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were separated. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was your other brother, while you were studying?

speaker: interviewee
text: The other?

speaker: interviewer
text: You had a brother named Michael.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. He was the oldest, was nine years older. And he finished the Russian gymnasium. Because at that time when he entered school, were no Jewish gymnasium. He was in the Russian gymnasium. And he finished in 1916, I think. And he went, again, as-- he went for year to the polytechnic in Warsaw, to study engineering. And he was there-- this was-- that time was not yet independence of Poland, 1916. And I think after a-- a year or-- or two-- I don't remember, I was too young-- for which reason probably it wasn't good enough, the-- the polytechnic, I don't know-- he went to study in Prague, Czechoslovakia. And at the time when I-- when he finished, he couldn't return to Poland. Because he also was not a citizen. And they blamed him that there-- when-- when there was the War of Independence in Poland, the war with Russia, after the end of the First World War, He was sitting in-- in Prague and studying when they were fighting on fronts. And this was partly a punishment. And partly he wasn't a citizenship-- a citizen. So he couldn't come. And in Prague, he had a hard time because he had no right to work. And I know my parents tried the best to do-- they went for-- for the people who know people, to get him-- to give the-- the permission he should come. But it took a long time. And I was, that time, already in France. And on the way to France now, I remind myself, we stooped for a day or two in Prague, with my brother, to-- to-- to see him. I hardly remembered him. But this what it was. And later in the-- I don't remember which year. Because when I came back for good, he was already home.

speaker: interviewer
text: He came home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, he was an engineer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me take you back. You were in Paris. And you were studying. And you stayed in Paris for how many years?

speaker: interviewee
text: Till 1933, from 1927. Because '25 through '26, I was in Krakow. From '26 to '27, I was in Rouen. And later I came to Paris. And this was my place to study. And I finished in 1903-- '33.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you work in any of the hospitals as--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah. Over there it's different than it is, let's say, here. Over there, the-- half a day in the morning were no classes and no laboratories, nothing, hospitals. And you have to go, be there. Everything else, that-- the theoretical there-- was in the afternoon. So in the morning it was always a hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: You made rounds.

speaker: interviewee
text: Rounds, everything. And we changed. We had obligatory to go here, there, which subjects.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there anything that you liked more than the other specialties?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was too fast to-- to-- to think too much. We had too much to do. At that time, I didn't think what I will do, what will be my speciality? No.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do in the evenings?

speaker: interviewee
text: We studied a lot. We will go out. I would go out. Later came one-- a-- a doctor from Italy. He finished. It was already in the latest year. So he was older than we are. And he took us out to different meeting-- I was going to some meeting. There were antisemitic excesses in France too, in-- in-- in Romania.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they were meetings to-- protest meetings. So he took us. On my own, I wouldn't do it. So we got a little bit involved.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I remember, I went to one meeting. And I had the-- the-- the-- the-- the honor to hear Jabotinsky. Yeah. So-- and we went to show, to a movie, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go dancing?

speaker: interviewee
text: This we went in group-- yeah. There was a-- students' dance, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: But it was all around the student life and the university?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only, yeah. I didn't meet any other-- other private people, businesspeople, rich people, no. Only students.

speaker: interviewer
text: You never went to a synagogue to meet with the people from the--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. I tell you that too, I'm ashamed to say, it was in the-- I didn't-- sometimes didn't even know when was Yom Kippur. I found out later. Because I wasn't particularly interested. And then the [INAUDIBLE] Maybe the men, the boys did. They didn't tell us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened in 1933, when you finished your medical studies?

speaker: interviewee
text: I applied to-- to come back to Poland. And to-- I had to pass the State Board in Poland, like in any other place, later, here. So I went back. I was admitted the first year, which I was lucky. I had many lucky events in my life. Because they admitted only a certain percentage every year. And there were many who applied. And I applied. I was admitted the first year. So I didn't have to wait. And I was called from home. My brother called me. I have to come immediately and-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --to report, because I was admitted. So I was admitted in Warsaw, to pass the State Board Examinations.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did that consist of, a writing exam?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, oral. Which was even worse. Because if my name comes up and my looks come out, and here I have a little bad experiences.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about those experiences.

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh-huh. There was one-- for instance-- professor. I remember his name because I never had anything to do with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE], whatever. When I didn't even say a word. So he right away said-- we were a group of students. And when it came my turn, he said, on a she, not-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the-- the name. But she has flies in her head. She thinks a lot of herself because she studied abroad. I was shivering, [LAUGHS] so he tells me that I have flies in my head. But he wanted to-- to drill me. But it didn't, I passed the examination.

speaker: interviewer
text: He said that to you because you were a Jew?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, sure, this what he said-- why he said it. A non-Jew doesn't pass this kind of examination. Because they study in-- in the country. And they go by normal channels. We came, though, like foreigners who had to pass a special [? examinate. ?] So a foreigner there was mostly a Jew. Because a French man or another didn't come to-- to live in France-- in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there was nothing you could do or say to him when he said that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't say. What can I say to him?

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened after you passed your exam?

speaker: interviewee
text: So after I passed examination, I had to be one year in a hospital. So that time already, I knew that I want to concentrate on pediatrics. So I did the obligatory, which is general medicine, which was included, internal medicine, surgery. But the rest I took already, to go to a children's hospital. And this is what I did it.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Lodz?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Lodz, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you lived at home with your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, always.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was the medical care good in-- in that hospital?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. They were a-- a lot of Jewish doctors. And their medicine was in a very high standard, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Private patients mostly?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, private and-- and if by insurance, and whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there a lot of Jewish patients?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Orthodox Jews also?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, whoever was sick.

speaker: interviewer
text: What--

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no discrimination, that they-- there were Jewish hospitals too, special. But I was not expected to learn in a Jewish-- you couldn't be in all places.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you started to work there, in the pediatric--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, after a year I started looking for some kind of a-- of a-- of a job to-- to exercise my medicine. So I got the first thing, I think was like here, a well baby clinic, a well baby clinic. And I opened my private practice. Well, no-- no, yeah, which but I didn't concentrate much. Because I later got a job in the union health center for workers. And this was in the periphery of the city. And I had to travel. I had a very-- from one end of the city, very, very far along, maybe an hour by tram, to go there. And I worked there until the end of-- the end of everything, when the war broke out.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your brothers also had come home, and they lived, also, with your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, this brother, for-- Isak, which started in-- in Nancy, in France, he remained over there. And he survived on Aryan paper. So now if you want to hear about my experience at the job which I had with the union health center.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was a section-- Lodz was a textile city. And there were a lot of fabric-- factories of textile. And this section was Polish there.

speaker: interviewee
text: There were no Jewish workers. I think they never admitted a Jew. That would kill them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So anyhow, I was the Jewish doctor over there. And that was normal relationship, to say. It was hard to please everybody. Because let's say if I want-- a-- a worker wanted to take off a few days. So I had to be very careful. Because there was always supervision, whether it's the right thing to do. So two things. I could always make them free, because it wasn't paid. So they didn't care. But they wanted more. If it was three days or four days, was the whole four days. So they bargained with me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And sometimes I couldn't do it. Sure, they wanted to have days off. So they threatened me. One-- one said, I will give you-- and everything, it is in English, it doesn't come to you though. And he said, you--

speaker: interviewer
text: You can say it in Polish.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Polish, I will give you with a knife in the-- in the ribs that you will remember me. And I also didn't react.

speaker: interviewee
text: What will I talk to him? He was illiterate or plain. And I will [? start-- ?] that will be a bigger fight [? than ?] I will win.

speaker: interviewee
text: But one experience was-- which is very interesting to mention. I was making-- making house calls after I saw the patients in the-- in the clinic. And I had a driver with the horse and buggy, they're always the same. And he took me around. This was long rides. Because one house from the other-- and it was already in the periphery, there were fields. And he took me from one house to another. There was always five, six house calls, more. Depends the season, winter. And he was very nice. One day he probably got drunk. Not probably, he was drunk. And all of a sudden, he get off from his seat and tries to sit near my seat. And I was scared. I was only 25, 26 years old all together. So I run off the-- the [? thing, ?] and I started running. And it wasn't so-- to-- to look for a house. And these houses, where my patients' living. So finally, I reached the house. And I knocked the door to let me in. I said, the driver is a little-- they didn't. And I ran to a next house, which again, he is after me. And the house is not one next to the other like here. It's far away. And I was knocking at many houses. Nobody let me in. But meanwhile, he sobered out. And he-- he-- he never did it before, and never did it later.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he-- so I told him, you know what? I wasn't able to make the calls. I said, take me back to the center. So we came to the center. So they recognized that something happened, I said. And there was another doctor where we were supposed to start the afternoon session to see patients in the place. So she was not a Jewish even, but very fine. And she said, you know what? I will take your house calls, which you didn't make and go with him. And you take my patients here. So meanwhile, on the place, I wasn't scared anymore. And I recovered from that. So that's how this incident-- So why do I mention it? These were my patients for-- for months, not years, I will say. And they knew that I am in danger. They didn't let me in. Because you were Jew?

speaker: interviewee
text: Probably. Their own doctor, they will take in. I was their doctor. But only to-- to-- to serve them. But when it came the moment when I needed them, they didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you never discussed that with them or with any higher authority?

speaker: interviewee
text: With whom? This is nobody. The doctor-- the-- my partner doctor was not Jewish. So she understood. She was nice. She said, I will-- I had-- we had to finish the patients.

speaker: interviewee
text: She will go on-- on the road. And I took her patients. And that's how we-- what I discussed. There was nobody to discuss. Because the nurses and everybody was not Jewish. So I'm going to complain? Did these patients come back to you after this incident?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: As if nothing happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. First of all, I don't remember who didn't let me in. Because I don't know exactly who lived in which house.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you work for that [INAUDIBLE] clinic?

speaker: interviewee
text: Two, three years, whatever it is. Because it wasn't too much, in '39 was then the beginning of the war. So everything was finished, and goodbye.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know from the papers or from the radio that things are getting worse and worse, that Hitler came to power?

speaker: interviewee
text: I knew because when I was in Warsaw and I was passing my state board examination, I-- well, met over there two doctors who studied in Vienna. And they knew more because they had relationships with Vienna. And one even went over there to visit somebody. They were from Galicia, both. And she came back. And this was in '34. And she told us what's going on, the beating of Jews and all. But I hardly could believe that this is true. But I believed her, that she doesn't say a lie. You know, but you know, again, I was studying all the time. And I knew what's going on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever discuss with your family that you should maybe leave Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. There was no question for us to leave. Because we didn't have the financial means to go. So where will we go?

speaker: interviewer
text: So what will be with all-- what will be with us? The-- of leaving, for that time, you had to have somebody, a passport. We were not in this category, so high, to think of it. But did people talk about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Quietly, things are getting worse?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this is what--

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were watching the news and-- the-- the listening to the radio, reading the paper. But we were-- it was [INAUDIBLE]. And they brought from Germany, when the-- the-- the laws came out, and they throw out the previous Polish citizen, they came.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I even went there, to-- to help out as a doctor. And people collected to help, clothes, food, you know? So we knew. And I knew. You saw other Jewish refugees coming into Lodz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah. Not exactly to Lodz, but to [INAUDIBLE] later, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: But life continued, more or less, normally?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your father still had the-- the business?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- no, my father was dead. My father died in 1933. So we didn't depend on our father. It was my brother the engineer. And I started working. And this how we lived. My mother was with us.

speaker: interviewee
text: But my father died in Paris, even, in 1933. I didn't mention that. Because you want to hear this? This is not important, maybe.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I worked in one hospital-- it's also interesting maybe too, that they-- one professor at the-- and over there wasn't like here. There were big groups. Because there were many students. We came to specialize from South America, and from all other countries. And I was a little student, a girl. And this was their methodology. And I was interested. So I will try to hide in the back.

speaker: interviewee
text: And all of the sudden, he calls me. And he says, mademoiselle, my name, I see that you have sad eyes. This is something. And now I observe you. Is something bothering you? So I said, yes, my father is sick. They somehow don't know or they know they cannot help him. And I am here, always, worrying.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said, when I am here, I come home. I'm afraid. Maybe there is a telegram that something happened.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he said to me-- I don't know why, but this what happened-- bring your father here. And I will see to help you. So I said, he is sick so many years.

speaker: interviewee
text: We don't have the money to bring him and to pay. He said, you bring him here, and I will take him as [FRENCH]. It means for scientific reasons.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I wrote home to my brother. It was again a difficult-- they didn't let in anybody abroad, because of-- the wisest not to take out money. We didn't have the money, is another story. But there was a very expensive passport. And it cost a lot of money.

speaker: interviewee
text: But my brother somehow managed that he got a passport like, for the sick, for treatment. And then one day I get a telegram that my mother and my-- accompanies my father. And they are coming tomorrow, let's say.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went with a friend of mine, a colleague, to the station to greet them. And I saw at the station that my father was half dead. And he was admitted to this professor's department.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he sent in an internist and this. He had then a special kind of anemia. But it was already not the beginning. It was late. And after five days, he died.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Paris?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Paris.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you bury him?

speaker: interviewee
text: This what I wanted to say. Again, I was 20-- not 25, almost 25. It was '30-- in '33, when I finished.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I had a colleague, a student, a doctor too. He was older than I. And he was, for-- for making a living, to make something, he worked for a Jewish paper. He was a journalist. So I went to him. He knew. And I said, will you help me? I have no money to send my father back, a body. So he somehow arranged that I didn't have to pay, that they-- they gave me a-- a grave in the Jewish cemetery. This what he did for me and my mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this was Passover. So this was a time when you don't even sit shiva. So I don't even have the recollection. There was no family, no visiting, nobody. I was here with my mother. My brother came from France for this, for the funeral. And after a few days, my mother went home.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old was your father when he died?

speaker: interviewee
text: About 67, something like that. I don't know. So this was an experience, a good one, a very good one, that such a professor stops me, and I was-- who was I, with-- you know? A little student from Poland, he didn't know even, who I am.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your father has a grave?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were back-- I'm taking you back to when you were working in Lodz, and you were working in a hospital. And you said that you [? were ?] reading in the papers and hearing on the radio that things are getting worse. When was the first time that you really felt there was a big danger for you personally, and for your mother, and for your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: When the war broke out. But I want to mention before, if it-- if you want in chronological reading, two months before the war, I got married to a man whom I dated and we courted for some times.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we didn't think even, of the war. Whatever happens, so what? It's a war. What-- I don't know, tell you the truth.

speaker: interviewee
text: We got married in June, '39. It is one day where Jews are allowed to get married. So this was the day.

speaker: interviewer
text: Lag BaOmer.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know, now even. Yes, so this was-- Lag BaOmer, is it? So we got married this day. And now the war broke out. And my new husband-- my husband, this is, was mobilized. And he left to the--

speaker: interviewer
text: In the Polish army?

speaker: interviewee
text: --to the Polish army. And now, by the way, he never came back. Now, when the war broke out, and this is-- he was supposed to report the first of October, September, when it is?

speaker: interviewer
text: September.

speaker: interviewee
text: September? September. I was already out of work. Because it was already the clinic was finished, out, no more, how it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that's all. So this-- that time I felt that-- what-- I went back to my mother and my brother. Because he was still home. And we didn't know it was such a [INAUDIBLE]. There was no question, what will I do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember, I had some medication to give in case of dehydration. And so a father who knew me came with a child, had diarrhea, whatever. It needed-- I could use this medication, to give it to him.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he wanted to pay me. So I said, I don't want any more, any money. Maybe he didn't have much. But I said, who needs, now, anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- we don't need anything. To me, it was the end of the world. Until it started getting, somehow, because we have to move to the ghetto. So I felt it.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to hear a little bit about your wedding. Was your husband a doctor also?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. He was a teacher and a principal of a school.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Majerkiewitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have a regular Jewish wedding?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. That-- that wasn't the time of-- that-- that-- for such things. And I am not going for the-- the-- the routine, what's going. It was we went to a rabbi and was a little reception. At that time, the style was like that. And anyhow, I didn't have the money for this. And there was, already, the atmosphere. There wasn't a wedding. It was-- the atmosphere [? wasn't ?] [? clear. ?] And anyhow, if it-- it would be, I didn't care for-- for these things.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you live in your own place?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we-- we rented, sublet, two rooms in someplace. Because-- and we got organized a little bit for the beginning. You know, we didn't know then will break out, the war, and everything will be over. And whatever I have, the little money, because my father didn't work so long. So and was dead so many years. And-- so this what I had. So later it was destroyed in one day. Because they throw me out from this apartment again, when-- when the Germans came.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your husband have a family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, he had a family, sisters and brothers. And-- but-- to-- it happened to be the same street we moved in, but by accident.

speaker: interviewer
text: Nobody survived from them?

speaker: interviewee
text: One brother survived because he lived in Israel. So I met him later. But I didn't know him at that time, yet. But from the others who were there, no. It were four, five sisters and two brothers.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old was your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: He-- well, I was 30-- at '33, how old was I? [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So he was 36.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. You never saw him again?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know where he was murdered?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, in Sobibor. So we will maybe come to that.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I found out from people. Because otherwise, you know--

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened September 1, 1939, when--

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- when he--

speaker: interviewer
text: --Poland--

speaker: interviewee
text: I know he left the house that time. And later, two days later, came already the German. It was one, two, three. And they throw me out from this apartment.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went to my mother. And later, my brother is an engineer. And he had a tour-- they-- they already-- this-- put which place will be the ghetto. So he was assigned from a-- a-- a factory or what, to build a hospital, to remodel for a hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he had a little push. And we got a-- a-- a room in the ghetto, which considered a good. Because considered he an engineer, I am a doctor. So there was a gas range and running water. This was-- Park Avenue, Fifth Avenue is nothing in comparison with this, what we got, running water and gas range. When did they establish the ghetto? Immediately?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, but we had to move in the beginning of January of 1940, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: Into what kind of an area of Lodz?

speaker: interviewee
text: The slums, the worst, which there was no sewer-- [? sewering ?] thing in it. It was-- it was no sanitary facilities. And no apartment.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they tell you you had to move? They went through the streets screaming it out? Or were there posters?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We knew that they-- they-- the Jewish-- what do you say? [NON-ENGLISH], how, what do you say?

speaker: interviewer
text: Community.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Jewish law said we-- the-- the Jews, we knew, have to move by that-and-that day. You have to be out from the city and in there. So everybody wanted to go, the earlier the better.

speaker: interviewer
text: What--

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was winter already.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you take with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very little. Because you had to have transportation. We didn't. And we took bedding probably, some, but I-- don't-- [? don't remember. ?] We took what we had, a couple pots. I imagine so. I saw people moving. They had a-- a pail with a broom, they moved. That's-- the main-- you didn't know what to do first.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a wall around the ghetto, or barbed wire?

speaker: interviewee
text: Later, yeah. There was-- there were wires, sure. We were completely, completely, hermetically isolated from the world.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the three of you lived in one room?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your brother--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, and mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: --your mother, and you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And other people in the same apartment?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, in the same house, yeah. And that were the slums. Because later, when everything was organized and all doctors were-- I wouldn't say mobilized, for sure. We were the first mobilized. Because everybody was sick. And everybody got an assignment, where to work.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was making-- finish? I was making house call. And I saw all these apartments. They were slums, like-- like-- like nothing. I never saw this before.
